---
title: "Maps and Sets in Java"
format: revealjs
code-font-size: "1.6em"
---

## Beyond the ArrayList

You already know `ArrayList` — a list that stores items **in order**, allows **duplicates**, and lets you access by **index**.

But sometimes you need something different:

. . .

> *"Does my list contain this username already?"*

> *"What is the phone number for 'Alice'?"*

> *"Make sure no value appears twice."*

. . .

For these questions, `ArrayList` works — but there are **better tools** for the job: **Sets** and **Maps**.

---

## What is a Set?

A **Set** is a collection that:

::: {.incremental}
- Stores **no duplicates** — adding something that's already there does nothing
- Has **no guaranteed order** (unlike a list)
- Lets you quickly ask: *"Is this value in the set?"*
:::

. . .

Think of a **set of student IDs** — each ID either is in the set or it isn't. You never want the same ID twice.

---

## Creating a HashSet

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
import java.util.HashSet;
import java.util.Set;

Set<String> enrolled = new HashSet<>();
```

. . .

::: {.callout-note}
We declare the variable as `Set<String>` (the interface) and create a `HashSet<String>` (the implementation). This is the standard Java style.
:::

---

## Core Set Methods

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
Set<String> enrolled = new HashSet<>();

enrolled.add("Alice");      // adds "Alice"
enrolled.add("Bob");
enrolled.add("Alice");      // duplicate — silently ignored!

System.out.println(enrolled.size());            // 2
System.out.println(enrolled.contains("Bob"));   // true
System.out.println(enrolled.contains("Zara"));  // false

enrolled.remove("Bob");
System.out.println(enrolled.contains("Bob"));   // false
```

| Method | What it does |
|--------|-------------|
| `add(x)` | Adds x; does nothing if already present |
| `contains(x)` | Returns true/false |
| `remove(x)` | Removes x if present |
| `size()` | Number of elements |

---

## Iterating Over a Set

Since *Sets have no index*, you use a **for-each loop**:

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
Set<String> fruits = new HashSet<>();
fruits.add("Mango");
fruits.add("Apple");
fruits.add("Banana");

for (String fruit : fruits) {
    System.out.println(fruit);
}
```

. . .

::: {.callout-warning}
The order of iteration is **not guaranteed** with `HashSet`. You might see `Apple`, `Banana`, `Mango` or any other order. If order matters, use `TreeSet` (alphabetical) or `LinkedHashSet` (insertion order).
:::

---

## Set Use Cases

::: {.incremental}
- **Checking for duplicates:** Add items one by one; if `size()` stays the same, you had a duplicate.
- **Membership testing:** Is this username taken? Is this word in the dictionary?
- **Removing duplicates from a list:** Dump an `ArrayList` into a `HashSet` and all duplicates vanish.
:::

. . .

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
// Remove duplicates from a list
ArrayList<Integer> nums = new ArrayList<>(List.of(3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6, 5));
Set<Integer> unique = new HashSet<>(nums);
System.out.println(unique);  // [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 9] — no duplicates
```

---

## What is a Map?

A **Map** stores **key-value pairs** — like a dictionary or a phone book.

::: {.incremental}
- Each **key** is unique (like a Set)
- Each key maps to exactly **one value**
- Values can be duplicated
- You look up values **by key**, not by position or value
:::

. . .

```
Key        →   Value
─────────────────────
"Alice"    →   "555-1234"
"Bob"      →   "555-9876"
"Carol"    →   "555-4444"
```

---

## Creating a HashMap

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
import java.util.HashMap;
import java.util.Map;

Map<String, String> phoneBook = new HashMap<>();
```

The first type is the **key type**, the second is the **value type**.

. . .

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
Map<String, Integer> scores  = new HashMap<>();  // name → score
Map<Integer, String> idToName = new HashMap<>(); // ID → name
Map<String, Double>  prices   = new HashMap<>(); // item → price
```

---

## Core Map Methods

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
Map<String, Integer> scores = new HashMap<>();

scores.put("Alice", 95);     // add or update a key-value pair
scores.put("Bob", 82);
scores.put("Alice", 99);     // updates Alice's score to 99

System.out.println(scores.get("Alice"));       // 99
System.out.println(scores.get("Bob"));         // 82
System.out.println(scores.get("Zara"));        // null — key not found

System.out.println(scores.containsKey("Bob")); // true
scores.remove("Bob");
System.out.println(scores.size());             // 1
```

---

## Map Methods at a Glance

| Method | What it does |
|--------|-------------|
| `put(key, value)` | Adds or updates a key-value pair |
| `get(key)` | Returns value, or `null` if key missing |
| `containsKey(key)` | Returns true/false |
| `remove(key)` | Removes the pair with that key |
| `size()` | Number of key-value pairs |
| `getOrDefault(key, def)` | Returns value, or `def` if key missing |

. . .

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
// getOrDefault is safer than get when key might be missing
int score = scores.getOrDefault("Zara", 0);  // 0 instead of null
```

---

## Iterating Over a Map

Loop over **entries** (key + value pairs) using `entrySet()`:

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
Map<String, Integer> scores = new HashMap<>();
scores.put("Alice", 95);
scores.put("Bob",   82);
scores.put("Carol", 88);

for (Map.Entry<String, Integer> entry : scores.entrySet()) {
    System.out.println(entry.getKey() + " → " + entry.getValue());
}
```

. . .

You can also loop over just the keys or just the values:

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
for (String name  : scores.keySet())   { System.out.println(name); }
for (int   score  : scores.values())   { System.out.println(score); }
```

---

## Map Use Cases

::: {.incremental}
- **Frequency counting:** How many times does each word appear in a text?
- **Caching/lookups:** Map student ID → Student object for fast lookup.
- **Grouping:** Map course name → list of enrolled students.
- **Configuration:** Map setting name → setting value.
:::

. . .

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
// Frequency count example
String[] words = {"the", "cat", "sat", "on", "the", "mat", "the"};
Map<String, Integer> freq = new HashMap<>();

for (String word : words) {
    freq.put(word, freq.getOrDefault(word, 0) + 1);
}

System.out.println(freq);
// {the=3, cat=1, sat=1, on=1, mat=1}
```

---

## Choosing the Right Collection

| Question | Use |
|----------|-----|
| Need items in order, allow duplicates, access by index | `ArrayList` |
| Need unique items, fast membership check | `Set` / `HashSet` |
| Need to look up values by a key | `Map` / `HashMap` |
| Need a Set that stays in sorted order | `TreeSet` |
| Need a Map that stays in insertion order | `LinkedHashMap` |

---

## Sets and Maps in the Real World

::: {.incremental}
- **Social networks:** Your list of friends is a Set (can't friend the same person twice). A DM inbox is a Map from username → messages.
- **Spell checkers:** The dictionary is a `Set<String>` — fast to ask "is this word valid?"
- **Games:** A Map from player name → score keeps the leaderboard. A Set tracks which rooms you've visited.
- **Web servers:** A Map from URL path → handler function routes requests.
:::

---

## Summary

::: {.incremental}
- A **Set** is an **unordered collection of unique values**.
  - `add()`, `contains()`, `remove()`, `size()`
  - Use when you need uniqueness or fast membership testing.

- A **Map** stores **key-value pairs** with unique keys.
  - `put()`, `get()`, `containsKey()`, `remove()`, `size()`
  - Use when you need to look something up by a key.

- Both are **generic** (`Set<T>`, `Map<K,V>`) — you choose the types.
- `HashSet` and `HashMap` are the standard implementations you'll use most.
:::
